title: Gigabyte

infobox.key: [Orders of magnitude of data](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(data) "Orders of magnitude (data)")

infobox.value: ([/ˈɡɪɡəbaɪt, ˈdʒɪɡəbaɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a multiple of the unit [byte](/wiki/Byte "Byte") for digital information. The [prefix](/wiki/SI_prefix "SI prefix") *[giga](/wiki/Giga- "Giga-")* means 109 in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI). Therefore, one gigabyte is one billion bytes. The unit symbol for the gigabyte is **GB**.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Unit of digital information

This article is about a multiple of bytes. For the binary unit of measurement, see [gibibyte](/wiki/Gibibyte "Gibibyte"). For the company, see [Gigabyte Technology](/wiki/Gigabyte_Technology "Gigabyte Technology"). For other uses, see [Gigabyte (disambiguation)](/wiki/Gigabyte_(disambiguation) "Gigabyte (disambiguation)").

| [Orders of magnitude of data](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(data) "Orders of magnitude (data)") | | | | |

The **gigabyte** ([/ˈɡɪɡəbaɪt, ˈdʒɪɡəbaɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a multiple of the unit [byte](/wiki/Byte "Byte") for digital information. The [prefix](/wiki/SI_prefix "SI prefix") *[giga](/wiki/Giga- "Giga-")* means 109 in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI). Therefore, one gigabyte is one billion bytes. The unit symbol for the gigabyte is **GB**.

This definition is used in all contexts of science (especially [data science](/wiki/Data_science "Data science")), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), [business](/wiki/Business "Business"), and many areas of [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing"), including storage capacities of [hard drives](/wiki/Hard_disk_drive "Hard disk drive"), [solid-state drives](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive"), and [tapes](/wiki/Magnetic-tape_data_storage "Magnetic-tape data storage"), as well as [data transmission](/wiki/Data_transmission "Data transmission") speeds. The term is also used in some fields of computer science and information technology to denote 1073741824 (10243 or 230) bytes, however, particularly for sizes of [RAM](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory"). Thus, some usage of *gigabyte* has been ambiguous. To resolve this difficulty, [IEC 80000-13](/wiki/IEC_80000-13 "IEC 80000-13") clarifies that a *gigabyte* (GB) is 109 bytes and specifies the term *gibibyte* (GiB) to denote 230 bytes. These differences are still readily seen, for example, when a 400 GB drive's capacity is displayed by [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows") as 372 GB instead of 372 GiB. Analogously, a memory module that is labeled as having the size "1GB" has one [gibibyte](/wiki/Gibibyte "Gibibyte") (1GiB) of storage capacity.

In response to litigation over whether the makers of electronic storage devices must conform to Microsoft Windows' use of a binary definition of "GB" instead of the metric/decimal definition, the [United States District Court for the Northern District of California](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_California "United States District Court for the Northern District of California") rejected that argument, ruling that "the U.S. Congress has deemed the decimal definition of gigabyte to be the 'preferred' one for the purposes of 'U.S. trade and commerce.'"[[2]](#cite_note-Order_Granting_Motion_to_Dismiss-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

The term *gigabyte* has a standard definition of 10003 bytes, as well as a discouraged[[2]](#cite_note-Order_Granting_Motion_to_Dismiss-2) meaning of 10243 bytes. The latter binary usage originated as compromise technical jargon for byte [multiples](/wiki/SI_prefix "SI prefix") that needed to be expressed in a power of 2, but lacked a convenient name. As 1024 (210) is approximately 1000 (103), roughly corresponding to SI multiples, it was used for binary multiples as well.

In 1998 the [International Electrotechnical Commission](/wiki/International_Electrotechnical_Commission "International Electrotechnical Commission") (IEC) published standards for [binary prefixes](/wiki/Binary_prefix "Binary prefix"), requiring that the gigabyte strictly denote 10003 [bytes](/wiki/Bytes "Bytes") and [gibibyte](/wiki/Gibibyte "Gibibyte") denote 10243 bytes. By the end of 2007, the IEC Standard had been adopted by the [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE"), [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and [NIST](/wiki/NIST "NIST"), and in 2009 it was incorporated in the [International System of Quantities](/wiki/IEC_80000-13 "IEC 80000-13"). Nevertheless, the term gigabyte continues to be widely used with the following two different meanings:

### Base 10 (decimal)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Base 10 (decimal)")]

* 1 GB = 1000000000 bytes (= 10003 B = 109 B)

Based on powers of 10, this definition uses the prefix giga- as defined in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI). This is the recommended definition by the [International Electrotechnical Commission](/wiki/International_Electrotechnical_Commission "International Electrotechnical Commission") (IEC).[[4]](#cite_note-NIST-4) This definition is used in [networking](/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network") contexts and most [storage media](/wiki/Storage_media "Storage media"), particularly [hard drives](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive"), [flash](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory")-based storage,[[5]](#cite_note-sandisk_gigabyte-5)[[6]](#cite_note-kingston_gigabyte-6) and [DVDs](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), and is also consistent with the other uses of the [SI prefix](/wiki/SI_prefix "SI prefix") in computing, such as [CPU clock speeds](/wiki/Megahertz#Megahertz_in_computing "Megahertz") or [measures of performance](/wiki/FLOPS "FLOPS"). The file manager of [Mac OS X](/wiki/Mac_OS_X "Mac OS X") version 10.6 and later versions are a notable example of this usage in software, which report files sizes in decimal units.[[7]](#cite_note-Apple_Inc-7)

### Base 2 (binary)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Base 2 (binary)")]

* 1 GiB = 1073741824 bytes (= 10243 B = 230 B).

The binary definition uses powers of the base 2, as does the architectural principle of [binary](/wiki/Binary_code "Binary code") [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer").
This usage is widely promulgated by some [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system"), such as [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows") in reference to [computer memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory") (e.g., [RAM](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory")). This definition is synonymous with the unambiguous unit [gibibyte](/wiki/Gibibyte "Gibibyte").

## Consumer confusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Consumer confusion")]

Since the first disk drive, the [IBM 350](/wiki/IBM_350 "IBM 350"), disk drive manufacturers expressed [hard drive](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive") capacities using decimal prefixes. With the advent of gigabyte-range drive capacities, manufacturers labelled many consumer [hard drive](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive"), [solid-state drive](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") and [USB flash drive](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive") capacities in certain size classes expressed in decimal gigabytes, such as "500 GB". The exact capacity of a given drive model is usually slightly larger than the class designation. Practically all manufacturers of hard disk drives and flash-memory disk devices[[5]](#cite_note-sandisk_gigabyte-5)[[6]](#cite_note-kingston_gigabyte-6) continue to define one gigabyte as 1000000000bytes, which is displayed on the packaging. Some operating systems such as [Mac OS X](/wiki/Mac_OS_X "Mac OS X"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS"), [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Ubuntu](/wiki/Ubuntu "Ubuntu"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) and [Debian](/wiki/Debian "Debian")[[10]](#cite_note-10) express hard drive capacity or file size using decimal multipliers, while others such as [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows") (including [Windows Phone](/wiki/Windows_Phone "Windows Phone")) report file size using binary multipliers. This discrepancy causes confusion, as a disk with an advertised capacity of, for example, 400 GB (meaning 400000000000bytes, equal to 372 GiB) might be reported by the operating system as "372 GB".

For [RAM](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory"), the [JEDEC memory standards](/wiki/JEDEC_memory_standards "JEDEC memory standards") use *IEEE 100* nomenclature which quote the gigabyte as 1073741824bytes (230 bytes).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The difference between units based on decimal and binary prefixes increases as a [semi-logarithmic](/wiki/Semilog "Semilog") (linear-log) function—for example, the decimal kilobyte value is nearly 98% of the kibibyte, a [megabyte](/wiki/Megabyte "Megabyte") is under 96% of a mebibyte, and a gigabyte is just over 93% of a gibibyte value. This means that a 300 GB (279 GiB) hard disk might be indicated variously as "300 GB", "279 GB" or "279 GiB", depending on the operating system. As storage sizes increase and larger units are used, these differences become more pronounced.

### US lawsuits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: US lawsuits")]

A lawsuit decided in 2019 that arose from alleged breach of contract and other claims over the binary and decimal definitions used for "gigabyte" have ended in favour of the manufacturers, with courts holding that the legal definition of gigabyte or GB is 1 GB = 1,000,000,000 (109) bytes (the decimal definition). Specifically, the courts held that "the U.S. Congress has deemed the decimal definition of gigabyte to be the 'preferred' one for the purposes of 'U.S. trade and commerce' .... The California Legislature has likewise adopted the decimal system for all 'transactions in this state'."[[2]](#cite_note-Order_Granting_Motion_to_Dismiss-2)

Earlier lawsuits had ended in settlement with no court ruling on the question, such as a lawsuit against drive manufacturer [Western Digital](/wiki/Western_Digital "Western Digital").[[12]](#cite_note-WesternDigital-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Baskin-2006-02-01-13) Western Digital settled the challenge and added explicit disclaimers to products that the usable capacity may differ from the advertised capacity.[[12]](#cite_note-WesternDigital-12)
Seagate was sued on similar grounds and also settled.[[12]](#cite_note-WesternDigital-12)[[14]](#cite_note-Seagate-14)

### Other contexts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other contexts")]

Because of their physical design, the capacity of modern computer random-access memory devices, such as [DIMM](/wiki/DIMM "DIMM") modules, is always a multiple of a power of 1024. It is thus convenient to use prefixes denoting powers of 1024, known as [binary prefixes](/wiki/Binary_prefixes "Binary prefixes"), in describing them. For example, a memory capacity of 1073741824bytes (10243 B) is conveniently expressed as 1 [GiB](/wiki/Gibibyte "Gibibyte") rather than as 1.074 GB. The former specification is, however, often quoted as "1 GB" when applied to random-access memory.[[15]](#cite_note-TS-GiB-15)

Software allocates memory in varying degrees of granularity as needed to fulfill data structure requirements and binary multiples are usually not required. Other computer capacities and rates, like [storage](/wiki/Computer_storage "Computer storage") hardware size, [data transfer rates](/wiki/Data_transfer_rates "Data transfer rates"), [clock speeds](/wiki/Clock_speed "Clock speed"), [operations per second](/wiki/FLOPS "FLOPS"), etc. are usually presented in decimal units. For example, the manufacturer of a "300 GB" hard drive is claiming a capacity of 300000000000bytes, not 300 × 10243 (which would be 322122547200) bytes.

## Examples of gigabyte-sized storage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Examples of gigabyte-sized storage")]

* One hour of [SDTV](/wiki/Standard-definition_television "Standard-definition television") video at 2.2 [Mbit](/wiki/Mbit "Mbit")/s is approximately 1 GB.
* Seven minutes of [HDTV](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television") video at 19.39 Mbit/s is approximately 1 GB.
* 114 minutes of uncompressed [CD-quality](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") audio at 1.4 Mbit/s is approximately 1 GB.
* A single-layer [DVD+R](/wiki/DVD%2BR "DVD+R") disc can hold about 4.7 GB.
* A dual-layered [DVD+R](/wiki/DVD%2BR "DVD+R") disc can hold about 8.5 GB.
* A single-layer [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") can hold about 25 GB.
* The largest [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") cartridge available on the market holds about 32 GB.
* A dual-layered [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") can hold about 50 GB.
* A triple-layered [Ultra HD Blu-ray](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray") can hold about 100 GB.

## Unicode character

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gigabyte&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Unicode character")]

The "gigabyte" symbol is encoded by [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") at code point U+3387 ㎇ SQUARE GB.[[16]](#cite_note-Unicode-U3300-16)
